content.problemStatement:
Here's a simple exercise that involves implementing a stack. 

In this exercise, you'll implement a basic stack to reverse a string using the stack's LIFO property.

TaskUpdate the functions `push()` and `pop()` within the class stack to output the reversed string.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a string S (0 ≤ |S| ≤ 100). The string may include spaces and punctuation. An empty line represents an empty string.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a line containing the reversed string of the corresponding input.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The single test case pushes each character of "Hello, World!" onto the stack and then pops them, producing the reversed string.
A single character is pushed and popped, resulting in the same character.
An empty string results in no characters being pushed; popping yields an empty output line.

hidden.label: edge case with spaces and punctuation

solution.approach: For each test case we simulate a stack using a fixed‑size array. We iterate over the characters of the input string and push each character onto the stack. Then we pop characters until the stack becomes empty, appending each popped character to the result. Because a stack follows LIFO order, the resulting sequence is the reverse of the original string. The algorithm runs in linear time with respect to the length of the string and uses O(N) auxiliary space for the stack.
solution.timeComplexity: O(L) per test case, where L is the length of the string (total O(T·L)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(L) auxiliary stack space per test case.
